title: Quiz: Real-World Risk Applications
description: Test your understanding of practical risk management applications

questions.question: Under Basel III, what is the typical confidence level used for regulatory VaR?
questions.explanation: Basel III uses 99% confidence level for VaR calculations, with a 10-day holding period for the trading book. This captures more extreme losses than the commonly used 95% VaR.

questions.question: What is the main characteristic of a risk parity portfolio?

options.text: Equal dollar weight to each asset
Equal risk contribution from each asset

questions.explanation: Risk parity portfolios are constructed so that each asset contributes equally to the total portfolio risk, not equal dollar amounts. This typically results in higher allocations to low-volatility assets.

questions.question: How is the risk contribution of an asset calculated?
questions.explanation: Risk contribution = wᵢ × MCTRᵢ = wᵢ × (∂σₚ/∂wᵢ). This measures how much of the portfolio's total risk is attributable to each asset's position.

questions.question: What replaced VaR in the Basel IV (FRTB) framework for market risk?
questions.explanation: The Fundamental Review of the Trading Book (FRTB/Basel IV) replaced VaR with Expected Shortfall at 97.5% confidence level, which better captures tail risk by averaging losses beyond the threshold.

questions.question: Buying put options is a cost-free way to hedge tail risk.
questions.explanation: False. Put options provide tail risk protection but come with an upfront premium cost. This cost acts as insurance - you pay for protection whether or not the tail event occurs.

questions.question: What does the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) measure in portfolio context?
questions.explanation: HHI = Σwᵢ² measures concentration. A higher HHI indicates more concentrated positions. The reciprocal (1/HHI) gives the 'effective number of assets' - how many equally-weighted positions would give the same concentration.

questions.question: What is the regulatory multiplier typically applied to VaR for capital calculations?
questions.explanation: Regulators typically apply a multiplier of 3.0 to 4.0 to the VaR calculation, depending on backtesting results. This accounts for model risk and ensures sufficient capital buffer.

questions.question: Which are components of an integrated risk management system? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: An integrated risk system includes risk metrics (VaR, CVaR), stress testing, and concentration analysis. No system can guarantee profits - risk management is about understanding and controlling potential losses, not predicting returns.
